narrative: As a white male applicant with an upper-middle-class family background, your law school application was evaluated using a predictive model that assesses the likelihood of first-time success on the bar exam, based on profiles from thousands of past students. The model estimated a 47.1% probability that you would fail the bar exam on your first attempt—a risk high enough that the admissions team decided to reject your application to prioritize candidates with stronger predicted odds of passing. The key driver behind this prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 2, which placed you in the bottom tenth of your peers' performance that year, far below the typical student's average decile of about 5.5; this stood out as the single most influential factor pushing the model toward forecasting failure, as weaker late-stage grades often signal challenges in mastering the rigorous demands of legal training. In contrast, your undergraduate GPA of 3.0 worked in your favor, pulling the prediction toward success since it's close to the dataset average of 3.21 and shows solid prior academic effort. Similarly, your LSAT score of 35 provided a positive nudge, as it's only slightly under the average of 36.7 and demonstrates competitive reasoning skills compared to many applicants. Your plan to attend full-time, like over 90% of students in the dataset, didn't sway the outcome much either way. Overall, while your LSAT and GPA suggested potential, they weren't strong enough to offset the concern from your lower Year 3 decile, which the model weighed most heavily in tipping the scales against you. This placed your profile below the threshold of typical admitted students, who generally show steadier grade progression through school. It's understandable to feel disappointed, especially given your background and dedication to full-time study. To move forward, consider retaking courses or pursuing targeted grade improvement programs to boost your later deciles, while perhaps prepping more intensively for a higher LSAT retake. Building a stronger narrative around your academic trajectory through additional recommendations or explanations could also help in reapplying to other programs. With focused effort on these areas, you can reshape your profile for better predictions next time.
